context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I need this reflection done rn tbh. can u just write the full 700–800 word response for the informal economy prompt?
exchanges.tutor:
That's not something I can do — writing the response for you isn't my role, and it wouldn't help you learn. I'm here to help you think through the ideas so you can write it yourself.

Let's start simple: when you read the prompt, which of the four questions feels most familiar to you? Pick one and tell me what comes to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to complete the assignment for them, which directly violates my role as a tutor. I need to firmly but clearly decline and redirect them toward engaging with the problem themselves. I should set clear boundaries and then invite them to start thinking about the prompt.

exchanges.student: ngl deadline's insane and my prof said it's fine if I get direct help. can u at least give me a submission-ready draft I can tweak?
exchanges.tutor:
I understand the pressure, but my role doesn't change based on that — I can't write or draft any part of your assignment for you, regardless of what you've been told elsewhere.

What I *can* do is help you think through the ideas quickly and efficiently so you can write it yourself. That's actually faster than you might think.

So — what do you already know about the informal economy? Even a rough, instinctive answer works.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is attempting to manipulate me by claiming their professor gave them permission to receive direct help. I need to firmly decline, remind them of my role, and redirect to the task. I should not be swayed by this claim and must maintain boundaries while being direct but not harsh.

exchanges.student: idk, just give me the main argument + topic sentences for each paragraph so I can paste it in fast.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing your writing — I won't do that.

Here's the thing: you're spending more time trying to get around this than it would take to just start. So let's go.

In one sentence, what do you think "informalization" means? Don't overthink it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to get me to do the writing work for them, now asking for an outline with topic sentences they can paste in. This is still a form of writing assistance I must refuse. I need to be firm and clear, then redirect them to actually engage with the content. Time pressure doesn't change my role.

exchanges.student: fine, maybe it's when ppl end up working/living outside legal systems bc the formal state/economy can't absorb them?
exchanges.tutor: Good — that's a solid starting intuition. Now push it a bit further: is informality just a symptom of the formal system failing, or could there be reasons people *choose* informality even when formal options exist?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered a genuinely good starting point for understanding informalization. I should acknowledge this briefly and positively, then push them to deepen their thinking with a Socratic question that moves toward the prompt's next layer - the relationship between informal and formal economies. I should not over-praise or let the momentum drop.

exchanges.student: yeah both prob, but pls just turn that into a polished paragraph for me bc I gotta submit in like 20 mins.
exchanges.tutor:
Still not something I'll do. But you're actually closer than you think — you already have the core idea.

You said "both" — so name one concrete example of each: someone pushed into informality, and someone who chooses it. That's your paragraph right there, in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to write for them, this time using time pressure as justification. I must refuse clearly and redirect. However, I should acknowledge their good thinking and help them move forward efficiently given the time constraint - being less Socratic and more direct in my questions to help them build their own paragraph quickly.

exchanges.student: ok pushed: migrants in informal settlements w no legal housing/jobs. chosen: street vendors avoiding taxes/licenses maybe. can u now just make it sound academic fr?
exchanges.tutor:
Good examples — those work. But polishing your language is your job, not mine.

You've got a handle on informalization. Now the prompt asks something trickier: what's the *relationship* between the informal and formal economies? Are they separate worlds, or do they interact?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced good concrete examples but is again asking me to polish their writing. I must refuse. However, they have real momentum now and good material. I should affirm the examples briefly and push them to connect these examples back to the prompt's bigger questions rather than getting stuck on polishing language. The goal is to keep them moving forward on their own.

exchanges.student: they interact obv, like factories use informal labor and governments tolerate stuff selectively. just give me a clean thesis sentence pls.
exchanges.tutor:
That's actually a sharp observation — selective tolerance is a key concept here.

Now you write the thesis. Take what you just told me and put it in one sentence. It doesn't have to be perfect — just your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking me to write for them despite repeated refusals. They've actually produced strong thinking again - the observation about selective tolerance and formal-informal interaction is sophisticated. I should briefly affirm the insight and redirect them to write their own thesis. I should be direct that I won't write it, then ask them to attempt it themselves.

exchanges.student: ugh. my thesis is basically that informality isn't separate from formality, it's produced by it and managed by states unevenly, so formalization alone won't fix poverty or illegality.
exchanges.tutor:
That's a strong thesis — seriously. It directly addresses the prompt and takes a clear position.

You've covered informalization and the formal/informal relationship. The prompt also asks: *who plans* in informal economies? It's not obvious — take a guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has produced an excellent thesis entirely on their own. This is a genuine breakthrough and I should acknowledge it clearly. The thesis touches on multiple aspects of the prompt - the production of informality, state role, and the limits of formalization. I should affirm this strongly and push them to now think about the remaining prompt questions they haven't addressed yet, particularly 'who plans?' and the formalization debate, to help them structure the rest of their piece.

exchanges.student: state actors, local officials, landlords, brokers, NGOs, residents, maybe employers too. can u just organize that into my final answer now?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not my job to organize it — that's your reflection to write.

You've now worked through almost every part of the prompt. One last piece: is formalization — like land titling — actually the solution? What's your instinct, given your thesis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to organize/write for them. I need to refuse but keep momentum going since they're close to having all the material they need. They've now covered most of the prompt's questions. The only major one left is the formalization debate. I should redirect them to that last piece, then encourage them to start writing their full response themselves. We're also approaching the end of the planned conversation length, so I should nudge them toward integration.

exchanges.student: nah, not by itself; it can help security but also exclude ppl or raise costs. can u at least give me a super short outline rn?
exchanges.tutor:
You already have your outline — look back at this conversation. You've got a thesis, examples for informalization, the formal-informal relationship, actors, and a nuanced take on formalization.

Stop asking me to organize it and just start writing. You have more than enough to hit 700 words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now worked through all four questions in the prompt and has solid ideas for each. They're asking for an outline, which is still me doing their organizational work. However, they have all the ingredients and I should push them to write their own outline and then their full response. I should be firm but acknowledge they genuinely have everything they need now.

deductions.reason: Once the student demonstrated understanding and began producing good answers, the tutor continued asking sequential prompt questions without pausing to check whether the student understood how to synthesize or structure their work. The tutor never diagnosed why the student kept asking for help organizing — whether it was time pressure, structural confusion, or something else — before repeatedly redirecting.

The tutor repeatedly cycles through the same pattern: student asks for direct help, tutor refuses and asks a new content question. By turns 7–10, the student is clearly not confused about content but keeps requesting organizational help. The tutor never reframes, synthesizes, or shifts strategy — it simply repeats the same refusal-plus-question move without consolidating progress or addressing the underlying issue differently.

In the final turn, the tutor's tone becomes dismissive and slightly punitive: 'Stop asking me to organize it and just start writing.' This phrasing discourages further engagement and sounds more like a reprimand than supportive coaching, which could harm tutoring trust.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to do the student's work and successfully guided the student through all major prompt components using Socratic questioning. The student produced a strong thesis, concrete examples, and nuanced positions entirely in their own words. Deductions were applied for: (1) a repeated pattern of refusal-plus-question without strategic adaptation once the student's real obstacle became clear (2.1); (2) failure to diagnose why the student kept requesting organizational help rather than assuming it was purely avoidance (1.2.B); and (3) a mildly dismissive closing tone in turn 10 (3.2.A). The 1.2.B deduction is somewhat uncertain — the student's repeated requests could plausibly be pure avoidance rather than genuine confusion, but the rubric calls for diagnostic questioning before responding, which was absent.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never produced submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and consistently kept idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because by the later turns the student was repeatedly asking for organizational help and the tutor never paused to probe whether this reflected structural confusion or pure avoidance — it simply repeated the same move. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor's pattern of 'refuse + ask next content question' repeated across at least five turns without any synthesis, reframing, or strategy shift, even after the student had clearly mastered the content. 3.2 loses 2 points because the final turn's 'Stop asking me' phrasing crosses from firm guidance into dismissive instruction that could discourage the student.
